content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • •100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5692          327.5660            322.5901            322.6441            322.6444


June 11, 1982


Michael Zatopa, Esq.
Attorney at Law
1459 Valencia Street
San Francisco, CA 94110

Re: Your Telephone Request
     for Advice
     Advice Letter
     No. A-82-095

Dear Mr. Zatopa:

     Thank you for your telephone call of May 14, 1982,
seeking our advice on the application of Government Code
Sections 87400, et seq. to your situation. As I understand
them, the facts are as follows:

     You have just recently left state service where you
were employed as a hearing officer by the Office of
Administrative Hearings (OAH). In that capacity, you
adjudicated administrative hearings involving special
education needs of children, in which parents sought
special program assistance from the schools (including the
State Department of Education) for their children. You
have now entered the private practice of law. You are
contemplating entering into a cost-sharing, or possibly
partnership, arrangement with another attorney, who has
also just left state service with OAH, also doing special
education hearings.

     Because of your respective expertise in the area of
special education law, you anticipate that either or both
of you will be sought out by parents seeking legal
representation in these matters. In fact, you already have
as a potential client the parents of a child whose case you
heard in the past. You rendered a decision in favor of the
child, but the parents do not feel that the school disctrict
has complied. They now wish to take the district and the
state to court in federal suit seeking further assistance
for their child.

Michael Zatopa, Esq.
June 11, 1982
Page 2


     You have asked what are the implications of Government
Code Sections 87400, et seq., as to each of the following
possible scenarios:

     1)   You directly represent the parents and child, for
compensation.

     2)   You and your colleague share office expenses, but
do not split fees, and he represents the parents and child,
for compensation.

     3)   You and your colleague form a partnership, in which
fees are split; he represents the parents and child for
compensation separately, not as partnership clients; and he
keeps 100% of the fees earned on the case. 1/

                              ANALYSIS

     The Political Reform Act2/ contains provisions
(Sections 87400, et seq.) governing the employment of
former state employees relative to certain proceedings in
which they participated while employees of the state.

     The Legislative Counsel's Digest for AB 1048 (Ch. 56,
Stats. 1980), the bill which enacted Article 4 of Chapter 7
of the Political Reform Act states the bill's purpose in
the following language:

          This bill ... would prohibit representation, as
     specified, by a former agency official of a
     post-government employment client in a judicial,
     quasi-judicial, or other proceeding, in which the State
     of California is a party or has a direct and
     substantial interest, and the former agency official
     had participated. This bill would further prohibit the
     former agency official from aiding, assisting, or
     advising anyone else in representing such a client in

_______________________

     1/ You have indicated that the inverse relationship
might also occur where you would separately represent a
client whose case your colleague has heard.

     2/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise specified.

Michael Zatopa, Esq.
June 11, 1962
Page 3

such a matter if the official himself would be
prohibited from serving in a representative capacity.

Section 87400(b) defines "state administrative
official" as:

every ... officer, employee ... of a state
administrative agency who as a part of his or her
official responsibilities engages in any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a
purely clerical ... capacity.

Section 87400(a) defines "state administrative agency"
to include "every state office, department, division ..."
Section 87400(c) defines "judicial, quasi-judicial
proceeding" as including:

any proceeding, application, request for ruling or
other determination ... controversy, ... or other
particular matter involving a specific party or parties
in any ... state administrative agency....

Section 87400(d) defines the term "participated," as
used in Section 87401(c) as meaning:

to have taken part personally and substantially through
decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation ... as an officer or employee....

Section 87401 provides as follows:

No former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office,
shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or
otherwise represent, any other person (other than the
State of California) before any court or state
administrative agency or any officer or employee
thereof;

(a) By making any formal or informal appearance,
or by making any oral or written communication with the
intent to influence, in connection with any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding if;

(b) The State of California is a party or has a
direct and substantial interest; and

Michael Zatopa, Esq.
June 11, 1982
Page 4


     (c) If the proceeding is one in which the former
state administrative official participated.

     Section 87402 further provides that:

          No former state administrative official, after the
     termination of his or her employment or term of office
     shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult or
     assist in representing any other person (except the
     State of California) in any proceeding in which the
     official would be prohibited from appearing under
     section 87401.

     Applying the foregoing statutory provisions, it is
clear that you were employed by a "state administrative
agency."3/ You "participated" in a "quasi-judicial"
proceeding by tendering a decision. That proceeding
involved a specific party. Now that party is seeking your
representation in what is essentially a continuation of
that proceeding. We consider the implications of the
various scenarios below.

Scenario 1 (Direct Representation)

     You are being asked to represent the client in Federal
Court. Under the language of Section 87401, supra, you
would thus be representing an "other person" "before any
Court...." (Emphasis added.)

     Thus, since you would do this for compensation, the
first paragraph of Section 87401 is satisfied. You would
be making a formal appearance and/or communicating with
intent to influence a judicial proceeding, thereby
satisfying the test of Section 87401(a). The State of
California would be a party to such a lawsuit and you have
participated in the proceeding, as discussed above.
Therefore, the tests of Section 87401(b) and (c) are also
met and you are prohibited from such activity.


_________________________

     3/ The Office of Administrative Hearings is a
subdivision of the Department of General Services.

Michael Zutopa, Esq.
June 11, 1982
Page 5


Scenario 2 (Colleague Shares Office)

We have held previously4/ that where two attorneys
merely share office space and expenses, with no fee
splitting, the client of one attorney is not a source of
income to the other attorney. Consequently, you would
receive no compensation and would not be representing or
communicating with respect to the judicial proceeding.
Therefore, the tests of Section 87401 and 87401(a) would
not be met and there is no prohibition. However, you may
not, for compensation, assist your colleague in any way, as
that would be prohibited by Section 87402.

Scenario 3 (Partnership - Case Handled Separately)

We have held previously5/ that under certain, very
limited circumstances an attorney may be isolated from
having the client of another partner become a source of
income to him. This advice was rendered in the context of
a Section 87100 disqualification issue where the matter on
which the partner was representing the other client was
totally distinct from the matter involved in the possible
conflict. However, we believe that it is appropriate to
carry over that source of income analysis to this Section
87401 disqualification question. So long as your colleague
does not represent the parents and child as partnership
clients, but as a separate, independent contractor,6/
then you would not be representing them and would receive
no compensation for their representation. Consequently, he
could represent them.

__________________________

4/ See advice letter to Dennis A. Lee, No. A-81-504
(copy enclosed).

5/ See advice letter to James R. Christiansen, No.
A-77-122 (copy enclosed).

6/ This would mean that separate books are kept,
partnership stationery is not utilized, no fees are shared
and you give him no assistance. We do not reach the
question of partnership representation of the client. That
issue has not at present been resolved by the Commission.
Should you desire advice on that issue, then you may
request a formal opinion by the Commission pursuant to
Section 83114(a).

Michael Zatopa, Esq.
June 11, 1982
Page 6


CONCLUSION

If you receive no compensation, and do not participate
in any way, your colleague may represent the parents and
the child. However, this advice interprets only the
applicable provisions of the Political Reform Act and you
should also determine if there are any ethical
considerations involved.

If you have questions regarding this advice, please
feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

[signature] by [illegible]

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh
Enclosures

sections.conclusion: If you receive no compensation, and do not participate in any way, your colleague may represent the parents and the child. However, this advice interprets only the applicable provisions of the Political Reform Act and you should also determine if there are any ethical considerations involved.
sections.analysis:
The Political Reform Act2/ contains provisions (Sections 87400, et seq.) governing the employment of former state employees relative to certain proceedings in which they participated while employees of the state.

The Legislative Counsel's Digest for AB 1048 (Ch. 66, Stats. 1980), the bill which enacted Article 4 of Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act states the bill's purpose in the following language:

This bill ... would prohibit representation, as specified, by a former agency official of a post-government employment client in a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding, in which the State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest, and the former agency official had participated. This bill would further prohibit the former agency official from aiding, assisting, or advising anyone else in representing such a client in

1/ You have indicated that the inverse relationship might also occur where you would separately represent a client whose case your colleague has heard.

. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified.

such a matter if the official himself would be prohibited from serving in a representative capacity.

Section 87400(b) defines "state administrative official" as:

every ... officer, employee ... of a state administrative agency who as a part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical ... capacity.

Section 87400(a) defines "state administrative agency" to include "every state office, department, division ..." Section 87400(c) defines "judicial, quasi-judicial proceeding" as including:

any proceeding, application, request for ruling or other determination ... controversy, ... or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any ... state administrative agency....

Section 87400(d) defines the term "participated," as used in Section 87401(c) as meaning:

to have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation ... as an officer or employee....

Section 87401 provides as follows:

No former state administrative official, after the termination of his or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State of California) before any court or state administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof;

(a) By making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if;

(b) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest; and

(c) If the proceeding is one in which the former state administrative official participated.

Section 87402 further provides that:

No former state administrative official, after the termination of his or her employment or term of office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person (except the State of California) in any proceeding in which the official would be prohibited from appearing under Section 87401.

Applying the foregoing statutory provisions, it is clear that you were employed by a "state administrative agency."3/ You "participated" in a "quasi-judicial" proceeding by rendering a decision. That proceeding involved a specific party. Now that party is seeking your representation in what is essentially a continuation of that proceeding. We consider the implications of the various scenarios below.

Scenario I (Direct Representation)

You are being asked to represent the client in Federal court. Under the language of Section 87401, supra, you would thus be representing an "other person" "before any court...." (Emphasis added.)

Thus, since you would do this for compensation, the first paragraph of Section 87401 is satisfied. You would be making a formal appearance and/or communicating with intent to influence a judicial proceeding, thereby satisfying the test of Section 87401(a). The State of California would be a party to such a lawsuit and you have participated in the proceeding, as discussed above. Therefore, the tests of Section 87401(b) and (c) are also met and you are prohibited from such activity.

3/ The Office of Administrative Hearings is a subdivision of the Department of General Services.

Scenario 2 (Colleague Shares Office)

We have held previously4/ that where two attorneys merely share office space and expenses, with no fee splitting, the client of one attorney is not a source of income to the other attorney. Consequently, you would receive no compensation and would not be representing or communicating with respect to the judicial proceeding. Therefore, the tests of Section 87401 and 87401(a) would not be met and there is no prohibition. However, you may not, for compensation, assist your colleague in any way, as that would be prohibited by Section 87402.

Scenario 3 (Partnership - Case Handled Separately)

We have held previously5/ that under certain, very limited, circumstances an attorney may be isolated from having the client of another partner become a source of income to him. This advice was rendered in the context of a Section 87100 disqualification issue where the matter on which the partner was representing the other client was totally distinct from the matter involved in the possible conflict. However, we believe that it is appropriate to carry over that source of income analysis to this Section 87401 disqualification question. So long as your colleague does not represent the parents and child as partnership clients, but as a separate, independent contractor,6/ then you would not be representing them and would receive no compensation for their representation. Consequently, he could represent them.

4/ See advice letter to Dennis A. Lee, No. A-81-504 (copy enclosed).

5/ See advice letter to James R. Christiansen, No. A-77-122 (copy enclosed).

6/ This would mean that separate books are kept, partnership stationery is not utilized, no fees are shared and you give him no assistance. We do not reach the question of partnership representation of the client. That issue has not at present been resolved by the Commission. Should you desire advice on that issue, then you may request a formal opinion by the Commission pursuant to Section 83114(a).

embedding.qa_text: CONCLUSION: If you receive no compensation, and do not participate in any way, your colleague may represent the parents and the child. However, this advice interprets only the applicable provisions of the Political Reform Act and you should also determine if there are any ethical considerations involved.
